sections.title: Basic overview
sections.text: Pot Marigold (Calendula officinalis) is a bright orange-yellow flowering plant often grown in gardens. Beyond its decorative and medicinal uses, it has long been valued in natural textile dyeing. Its petals contain pigments that produce warm, sunny tones on fabrics.

sections.title: Dyeing use
sections.text: In natural fabric dyeing, pot marigold is used to create soft orange and yellow shades. The petals are simmered in water to release their pigments, which can then be applied to natural fibers such as cotton, wool, or linen. The resulting color may vary depending on the use of mordants and dyeing conditions.

sections.title: Pigment and color
sections.text: The pigments in pot marigold come mainly from carotenoids, which are responsible for the plant’s bright orange and yellow hues. These pigments are released when the petals are heated in water during the dyeing process. The final color can shift depending on temperature, pH, and mordants, producing shades from golden yellow to deep orange. Pot marigold dye is appreciated for its warm, vibrant tones, though it may fade with prolonged light exposure or repeated washing.

sections.title: Plant
sections.text: Stinging Nettle (Urtica Dioica) is a common wild plant known for its fine hairs that can irritate the skin. Beyond its medicinal and culinary uses, it has also been used in traditional textile practices. The plant contains natural pigments that can be extracted to dye fabrics in soft green and yellow tones.

sections.title: Use
sections.text: In natural fabric dyeing, stinging nettle is valued for its ability to produce subtle, earthy colors. The leaves and stems are typically boiled to release the dye, which can then be applied to natural fibers like cotton, wool, or linen. The final shade depends on factors such as water quality, mordants, and dyeing time.

sections.title: Pigment
sections.text: The pigment in stinging nettle comes mainly from natural compounds such as chlorophyll, which gives the plant its green color. The resulting color can vary depending on temperature, pH, and the use of mordants, often producing shades from light green to yellow-green. Nettle dye is valued for its soft, natural tones, although the color may be less stable and can fade with exposure to light and repeated washing.

bottomImages.title: use of pigment in Czech folklor

sections.title: Basic overview
sections.text: Golden Rod (Solidago spp.) is a bright yellow-flowering plant commonly found in meadows and along roadsides. Besides its ornamental and medicinal uses, it has a long history in natural textile dyeing. The plant contains pigments that can impart warm yellow hues to fabrics.

sections.title: Dyeing use
sections.text: In natural fabric dyeing, Golden Rod is prized for producing rich, sunny yellow shades. The flowers and sometimes leaves are simmered in water to extract the pigment, which is then applied to natural fibers like wool, cotton, or linen. The final tone can be adjusted using different mordants and dyeing techniques.

sections.title: Pigment and color
sections.text: The yellow color in golden rod comes from flavonoid compounds, which are water-soluble pigments. These pigments are released when the plant material is heated in water. The resulting shades can range from soft gold to bright yellow, depending on temperature, pH, and mordants used. Golden rod dye is admired for its warm, vibrant color, though it may fade gradually with light exposure and repeated washing.

sections.title: Plant
sections.text: Beetroot (Beta vulgaris) is a root vegetable known for its deep red color and sweet, earthy taste. It is rich in vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants, and is often used in salads, juices, and soups. Beetroot has also been used in traditional medicine and is valued for supporting overall health. Its intense color makes it useful not only in food but also as a natural coloring agent.

sections.title: Use
sections.text: Beetroot can be used as a natural dye, producing shades of pink, red, and purple. To prepare the dye, the root is chopped and simmered in water to release its color. The liquid is then strained and used as a dye bath. Fabrics are usually treated with a mordant, such as alum or vinegar, to help the color bind, although beetroot dye tends to fade more quickly than other natural dyes.

sections.title: Pigment
sections.text: The pigment in beetroot comes from natural compounds called betalains, mainly betanin, which give the root its strong red color. These pigments are released when the beetroot is crushed or heated in water. The color can vary depending on temperature and pH, often shifting between red and pink tones. Beetroot pigment is appreciated for its bright, vibrant appearance, although it is less stable and more sensitive to light and washing.

sections.title: Plant
sections.text: Onion (Allium cepa) is a common vegetable used worldwide in cooking. It grows underground as a bulb and is known for its strong smell and flavor. Onions are rich in vitamins and antioxidants and have been used in traditional medicine for their antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. They are easy to grow and are an important part of many cuisines.

sections.title: Use
sections.text: Onion skins, especially the dry outer layers, are widely used as a natural dye. They produce warm shades of yellow, orange, and brown. To make a dye, the skins are simmered in water to release their color, and the liquid is then used as a dye bath. The fabric is often treated with a mordant, such as alum, to help the color last longer. The pigment in onion skins comes mainly from natural compounds called flavonoids, especially quercetin. These pigments are released when the skins are boiled in water.

sections.title: Pigment
sections.text: The resulting dye can vary in color depending on the type of onion and the mordant used. Onion pigment is valued for its earthy tones, good color fastness, and ability to create rich, natural shades on fabric.

bottomImages.title: Use of pigment in Czech folklor

sections.title: Plant
sections.text: Elderberry (Sambucus nigra) is a small tree or shrub known for its dark purple berries and white flowers. It has been used for centuries in traditional medicine, especially to support the immune system and help with colds and flu. The berries are commonly made into syrups, teas, and jams, but they must be cooked before eating, as raw berries can be toxic. Elderberry plants are also popular in nature for attracting birds and pollinators.

sections.title: Use
sections.text: Berries, often called elderberries, are small dark purple berries that grow on the elder tree after the flowers bloom. They can be used to create natural dyes for fabric, producing deep purples, blues, or burgundy tones depending on the mordant and fabric type. Pigment from elderberries comes from the natural anthocyanins in the fruit, which give the berries their deep purple to almost black color. This pigment can be extracted by gently crushing or simmering the berries in water to release their juice.

sections.title: Pigment
sections.text: The resulting liquid can be used as a natural dye or pigment for painting, fabric coloring, or ink-making. The color can vary depending on pH: acidic conditions produce reddish tones, while alkaline conditions shift the pigment toward blue or purple. Elderberry pigment is valued for its rich, organic hues and subtle, natural variations.

sections.title: Basic overview
sections.text: Virginia Creeper (Parthenocissus quinquefolia) is a climbing vine native to North America, known for its lush green leaves that turn bright red in autumn. Beyond its ornamental appeal, it has been used in traditional textile dyeing. The leaves and berries contain natural pigments that can color fabrics in red, purple, or blue tones.

sections.title: Dyeing use
sections.text: In natural fabric dyeing, Virginia Creeper is valued for producing rich autumnal colors. The berries are often simmered to extract red or purple pigments, while leaves can yield greenish to brownish tones. The extracted dye is applied to natural fibers like cotton, wool, or linen, and the final shade depends on mordants, pH, and dyeing time.

sections.title: Pigment and color
sections.text: The pigments in Virginia Creeper come mainly from anthocyanins in the berries and chlorophyll in the leaves. When heated in water, these compounds release colors ranging from red and purple to greenish-brown, depending on pH and mordant used. Virginia Creeper dye is prized for its warm, natural hues, though like many plant dyes, the colors may fade over time with light exposure or repeated washing.
